title: Carbon Pricing Comparison
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Side-by-side simulation comparing carbon tax and cap-and-trade mechanisms with adjustable policy parameters and long-term emissions projections

learningObjectives: Compare the mechanisms, advantages, and limitations of carbon tax and cap-and-trade systems
Evaluate how different policy parameters affect emissions reductions and economic costs
Explain the concepts of price certainty vs. quantity certainty in environmental policy
prerequisites: Basic understanding of supply and demand
Familiarity with greenhouse gas emissions and climate change

keyQuestions: What is the difference between a carbon tax and cap-and-trade?
Which system provides more certainty about total emissions levels?
How do individual factory cost curves affect the outcome of each policy?
Why might a government prefer one carbon pricing mechanism over the other?
commonMisconceptions: Students often assume that a higher carbon tax always leads to lower emissions, not recognizing that factories with very high abatement costs may simply pay the tax
Many students believe cap-and-trade means all factories must reduce equally, rather than trading permits so reductions happen where they are cheapest
Students may think carbon pricing eliminates all emissions rather than reducing them to an economically efficient level
assessmentOpportunities: Students predict which factories will reduce emissions under each policy before running the simulation
Students write a policy brief recommending one system with evidence from their experiments
Students explain why the permit price under cap-and-trade may differ from the tax rate needed to achieve the same total reduction
